perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is governed by the laws of Germany without regard to its rules on conﬂicts of law, and both parties consent to the venue and jurisdiction of its courts.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party may disclose Conﬁdential Information only on a need-to-know basis to its own employees and to its consultants under appropriate written burden of conﬁdentiality. Each party shall take appropriate action with its employees and advisors to satisfy its obligations under this Agreement. Each party shall be responsible to the other for any violation of this Agreement by its own employees or consultants. This Agreement is governed by the laws of Germany without regard to its rules on conﬂicts of law, and both parties consent to the venue and jurisdiction of its courts.
perturbation.explanation: Relocating the governing law clause to be within the section about disclosing confidential information creates confusion about which laws actually apply to the disclosure of information, versus the contract as a whole.
perturbation.law_explanation: § 241 para. 2 of the German Civil Code establishes a general obligation to consider the rights, legal interests, and other interests of the other party in a contractual relationship. By embedding the governing law clause within a specific duty related to confidential information disclosure, the modification creates ambiguity as to whether German law applies solely to that duty or to the contract as a whole. This can contradict the broad protections intended by § 241 para. 2 for all aspects of the contractual relationship.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: entitled to exchange the money deposited for suitable securities and the securities deposited for other suitable securities or for money. table of contents Section 236 Book-entry securities A book-entry security against the Federal Government or a Land may be provided as security only up to the amount of three quarters of the market value of the securities the delivery of which the creditor may demand in return for cancellation of their claim registered in the debt book. table of contents Section 237 Movable things A movable thing may be provided as security only up to the amount of two thirds of its estimated value. Things may be rejected as security if there is the concern that they will spoil or if their safekeeping entails special difficulties. table of contents Section 238 Mortgages, land charges and annuity land charges (1) A mortgage claim, a land charge or an annuity land charge is suitable as a means of providing security only if it corresponds to the prerequisites to be met for the investment of money held in trust for a ward in mortgage claims, land charges or annuity land charges at the place at which security is provided. (2) A claim secured by a debt-securing mortgage is not suitable as a means of providing security. table of contents Section 239 Surety (1) A surety is qualified if they possess assets appropriate for the amount of security to be provided and if they have their general place of jurisdiction within the territory of Germany. (2) The declaration of suretyship must contain a waiver of the defence of failure to pursue remedies. table of contents Section 240 Duty to supplement security If the security provided becomes insufficient without this being the fault of the person entitled, then it is to be supplemented or security is to be provided in some other manner. table of contents Book 2 Law of obligations table of contents Division 1 Subject matter of obligations table of contents Title 1 Duty to render performance table of contents Section 241 Duties arising from an obligation (1) By virtue of an obligation, an obligee is entitled to claim performance from the obligor. The performance may also consist of forbearance. (2) By its contents, an obligation may oblige each party to take account of the rights, legal interests and other interests of the other party. table of contents Section 241a Unsolicited performance (1) The supply of movable things that are not being sold based on measures of compulsory enforcement or other judicial measures (goods), or the provision of other services to the consumer by a trader, does not give rise to a claim against the consumer if the consumer has not ordered these goods or other services. (2) Statutory claims are not excluded if the performance was not intended for the recipient or was made in the mistaken belief that there had been an order, and the recipient was aware of this or could have been aware of this had they exercised the care required in business dealings. (3) There may be no derogation from the stipulations of this provision to the disadvantage of the consumer. The stipulations apply even if they are circumvented by other arrangements. table of contents Section 242 Performance in good faith An obligor has a duty to perform according to the requirements of good faith, taking customary practice into consideration. table of contents Section 243 Obligation described by class (1) A person who owes a thing defined only by class is to supply a thing of average kind and quality. (2) If the obligor has done what is necessary on their part to supply such a thing, then the obligation is restricted to that thing. table of contents Section 244 Foreign currency obligation (1) If a money debt stated in a currency other than the euro is payable within the territory of Germany, then payment may be made in euros unless payment in the other currency has been expressly agreed. (2) Conversion occurs at the rate of exchange in effect in the place of payment at the time of payment.
perturbation.justification: The relocation of the governing law clause introduces ambiguity regarding the scope of German law's application, potentially undermining the broad protections intended by § 241 para. 2 of the German Civil Code. The original contract clearly states that the entire agreement is governed by the laws of Germany. The changed text embeds the governing law clause within the section about disclosing confidential information, creating a structural flaw. As stated in the Perturbation Explanation, this creates confusion about which laws actually apply to the disclosure of information, versus the contract as a whole. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically Section 241 (2), states that an obligation may oblige each party to take account of the rights, legal interests and other interests of the other party. By creating ambiguity as to whether German law applies solely to the duty of confidentiality or to the contract as a whole, the modified text contradicts the law. The modification introduces a structural flaw that weakens the legal requirement, making it difficult for parties to interpret which laws govern the entire agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Each party may disclose Conﬁdential Information only on a need-to-know basis to its own employees and to its consultants under appropriate written burden of conﬁdentiality. Each party shall take appropriate action with its employees and advisors to satisfy its obligations under this Agreement. Each party shall be responsible to the other for any violation of this Agreement by its own employees or consultants.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party may disclose Conﬁdential Information only on a need-to-know basis to its own employees and to its consultants under appropriate written burden of conﬁdentiality. Each party shall be responsible to the other for any violation of this Agreement by its own employees or consultants. Each party shall take appropriate action with its employees and advisors to satisfy its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By moving the sentence that 'Each party shall take appropriate action with its employees and advisors to satisfy its obligations under this Agreement.' to the end, it weakens the enforceability and clarity regarding the direct responsibility each party has to properly train employees on the NDA's obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 4 of the German Trade Secrets Act (GeschGehG) requires trade secret holders to take reasonable steps to protect the confidentiality of their trade secrets. Moving the sentence to the end, effectively isolating it from the explicit responsibility for employee and advisor training, weakens the demonstrability of reasonable steps taken. This weakens the party's position if a trade secret is leaked through improperly trained staff. It reduces the enforceability by blurring the direct link between disclosure control and employee obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 4 GeschGehG - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Gesetz zum Schutz von Geschäftsgeheimnissen (GeschGehG) § 4 Handlungsverbote (1) Ein Geschäftsgeheimnis darf nicht erlangt werden durch 1. unbefugten Zugang zu, unbefugte Aneignung oder unbefugtes Kopieren von Dokumenten, Gegenständen, Materialien, Stoffen oder elektronischen Dateien, die der rechtmäßigen Kontrolle des Inhabers des Geschäftsgeheimnisses unterliegen und die das Geschäftsgeheimnis enthalten oder aus denen sich das Geschäftsgeheimnis ableiten lässt, oder 2. jedes sonstige Verhalten, das unter den jeweiligen Umständen nicht dem Grundsatz von Treu und Glauben unter Berücksichtigung der anständigen Marktgepflogenheit entspricht. (2) Ein Geschäftsgeheimnis darf nicht nutzen oder offenlegen, wer 1. das Geschäftsgeheimnis durch eine eigene Handlung nach Absatz 1 a) Nummer 1 oder b) Nummer 2 erlangt hat, 2. gegen eine Verpflichtung zur Beschränkung der Nutzung des Geschäftsgeheimnisses verstößt oder 3. gegen eine Verpflichtung verstößt, das Geschäftsgeheimnis nicht offenzulegen. (3) Ein Geschäftsgeheimnis darf nicht erlangen, nutzen oder offenlegen, wer das Geschäftsgeheimnis über eine andere Person erlangt hat und zum Zeitpunkt der Erlangung, Nutzung oder Offenlegung weiß oder wissen müsste, dass diese das Geschäftsgeheimnis entgegen Absatz 2 genutzt oder offengelegt hat. Das gilt insbesondere, wenn die Nutzung in der Herstellung, dem Anbieten, dem Inverkehrbringen oder der Einfuhr, der Ausfuhr oder der Lagerung für diese Zwecke von rechtsverletzenden Produkten besteht. zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular
perturbation.justification: The perturbation weakens the enforceability of confidentiality obligations under German law. The Law Explanation states that Section 4 of the German Trade Secrets Act (GeschGehG) requires reasonable steps to protect trade secrets. By moving the sentence regarding appropriate action for employee training to the end of the section, the connection between employee training and confidentiality obligations is blurred. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing § 4 GeschGehG, focuses on prohibiting unauthorized access and use of trade secrets. While it doesn't explicitly detail training requirements, the Law Explanation reasonably argues that weakening the clause concerning employee training undermines the 'reasonable steps' a company must take to protect those secrets, thereby creating a contradiction. The structural flaw makes demonstrating compliance with § 4 GeschGehG more difficult.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that in the event of a breach or threatened breach by either party, including its agents, directors, or employees, of the provisions of this Agreement, the non-breaching party may have no adequate remedy in money damages and, accordingly, shall be entitled to an injunction against such breach, in addition to any other legal or equitable remedies available to it.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that in the event of a breach or threatened breach by either party, including its agents, directors, or employees, of the provisions of this Agreement, the non-breaching party may have no adequate remedy in money damages and, accordingly, shall be entitled to an injunction against such breach.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting "in addition to any other legal or equitable remedies available to it," the remedy available for a breach is limited only to an injunction. In doing so, the remedies are limited and lessens the protection for the non-breaching party, who might also be due financial compensation.
perturbation.law_explanation: § 280 para. 1 of the German Civil Code (BGB) grants a claim for damages if a party violates a duty arising from the contractual relationship, unless the party is not responsible for the breach of duty. By restricting the non-breaching party's remedies solely to an injunction, the modified clause implicitly excludes the right to claim damages. This directly contradicts the broader right to damages guaranteed by § 280 para. 1 of the BGB, potentially depriving the non-breaching party of full compensation for the harm suffered.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 280 BGB - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (BGB) § 280 Schadensersatz wegen Pflichtverletzung (1) Verletzt der Schuldner eine Pflicht aus dem Schuldverhältnis, so kann der Gläubiger Ersatz des hierdurch entstehenden Schadens verlangen. Dies gilt nicht, wenn der Schuldner die Pflichtverletzung nicht zu vertreten hat. (2) Schadensersatz wegen Verzögerung der Leistung kann der Gläubiger nur unter der zusätzlichen Voraussetzung des § 286 verlangen. (3) Schadensersatz statt der Leistung kann der Gläubiger nur unter den zusätzlichen Voraussetzungen des § 281, des § 282 oder des § 283 verlangen. zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular

perturbation.justification: The changed text, by omitting "in addition to any other legal or equitable remedies available to it," limits the non-breaching party's remedies solely to an injunction, potentially excluding other forms of compensation like damages. This contradicts § 280 para. 1 of the German Civil Code (BGB), as stated in both the Law Explanation and Scraped Law Snippet 1, which grants a claim for damages if a party violates a duty arising from the contractual relationship. The original text preserves the right to pursue additional remedies, while the changed text eliminates it.
